genuine_difference: The current theory states the condition at a higher abstraction level across biological, institutional, computational, and organizational systems.
prediction: One-shot systems cannot accumulate reliable knowledge over time except by inheriting another system's retained state.
reason: Repeated sampling, feedback, recursive estimation, and interaction are core assumptions in existing learning, control, and cybernetic frameworks.
text: Cumulative knowledge requires repeated interaction between system and environment.

genuine_difference: The theory treats memory as substrate-neutral: parameters, records, genes, prices, practices, and institutions can all instantiate retention.
prediction: Systems without durable state cannot improve across cycles at the system level.
reason: The core idea is old, but the substrate-neutral organizational framing usefully prevents confusing human-readable archives with memory itself.
text: Cumulative knowledge requires retention: prior interaction must affect later behavior.

genuine_difference: The theory packages feedback alignment as a primitive axiom for any cumulative knowledge system, not only as a loss/reward design issue.
prediction: A system can have repeated interaction, retention, and feedback while converging to falsehood if feedback is biased, adversarial, or Goodharted.
reason: Misleading signals and bad rewards are well known; making truth-correlation a cross-domain axiom is a useful unification, not a clear discovery.
text: Feedback must be truth-correlated rather than merely present.

genuine_difference: The theory makes applicability boundaries a first-class requirement rather than a downstream caveat.
prediction: Systems that ignore scope will overgeneralize, transfer failures across domains, and mistake local adaptation for global knowledge.
reason: Domain restrictions, hypothesis classes, external validity, and paradigm-relative puzzles are established; the novelty is the operational insistence that scope must be retained with knowledge artifacts.
text: Reliable knowledge requires scope control: retained claims must carry conditions of transfer.

genuine_difference: The theory links cost pressure to organizational attention and research strategy, not just coding length or sample complexity.
prediction: When observation, computation, or communication is costless, compression pressure becomes optional rather than necessary.
reason: The cost-compression connection is classic; applying it uniformly to research organizations is a useful reformulation.
text: Nonzero cost makes compression, reuse, and prioritization epistemically relevant.

genuine_difference: The theory elevates stability of updates from an engineering property to a minimal axiom for cumulative knowledge.
prediction: Some systems satisfying interaction, retention, feedback, scope, and cost still fail through catastrophic forgetting, oscillation, or destructive self-modification.
reason: Every ingredient has prior art, but the exact necessity claim across all epistemic substrates appears less directly covered.
text: Cumulative knowledge requires update stability: learning must not destroy, invert, or endlessly oscillate around valid retained knowledge faster than correction can recover it.

genuine_difference: The schema is intentionally minimal and cross-substrate; it avoids assuming probabilities, symbols, rewards, agents, or explicit theories.
prediction: The same abstract schema should classify machine learners, evolution, institutions, markets, collectives, and symbolic reasoners.
reason: This is mostly a synthesis. It becomes novel only if it predicts failures outside the home domains of existing frameworks better than those frameworks do.
text: Any cumulative knowledge system can be represented as retained state, environment interaction, update operator, truth-correlated evaluation, scope relation, cost pressure, and stability constraint.

genuine_difference: The theory treats failures as reusable state, not just historical narrative or local lessons learned.
prediction: Organizations that preserve failed hypotheses should reduce duplicate exploration and improve time-to-falsification compared with equally active organizations that do not.
reason: Failed hypotheses, anomalies, and lessons learned are well established. The stronger artifact-level prediction is testable but not obviously unprecedented.
text: Preserved failures and counterexamples improve cumulative knowledge by preventing rediscovery and stabilizing criticism.

genuine_difference: The theory operationalizes question disappearance as a compression event in a research state.
prediction: Research progress often deletes ill-posed questions rather than simply answering them.
reason: The intellectual content overlaps Kuhn, MDL, and double-loop learning; the improved part is measurement and artifact tracking.
text: Compression through question refinement is a central route of cumulative understanding.
